contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Reject universal claims: choose from correctness, guarantees, memory, ordering, mutation, locality, key operations, and complete workload cost.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly replaces the heuristic "a hash map is always faster than sorting"?

options.text: Hashing is often expected-linear for equality state, but the preferred strategy depends on correctness, guarantees, memory, ordering, mutation, key costs, and the full workload.
Hashing dominates every sorting-based solution.
Sorting dominates every hash-based solution.
Both strategies always have identical costs.

feedback.reason: The false heuristic should be replaced by an explicit conditional decision rule.
feedback.details: Both families are useful when their mechanism matches the result contract and cost model.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_universal: Counterexamples include ordered output, unhashable keys, existing sorted order, tight memory, and adversarial guarantees.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_universal: Many equality-heavy workloads benefit from hashing.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_always: Their time, space, guarantees, and output structure differ.

items.prompt: Why is expected O(1) hash lookup not a universal latency guarantee?

options.text: Collisions, resizing, adversarial inputs, and implementation details can create slower operations.
Hash lookup is not a bit operation.
Sorting always has O(1) latency.
Hash tables never compare keys.

feedback.reason: Expected and worst-case analyses answer different operational requirements.
feedback.details: A system requiring deterministic bounds may prefer sorting or a protected hashing scheme.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.lookup_not_bit: That does not explain the guarantee boundary.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.keys_no_equality: Equality checks are part of collision resolution.

items.prompt: How can cache locality challenge the assumption that fewer expected hash operations always run faster?

options.text: Hash tables may perform indirect, irregular accesses, while sorting and scanning contiguous arrays can use caches efficiently.
Caches change O(n log n) into O(1).
Hashing has no memory accesses.
Sorting always accesses memory randomly.

feedback.reason: Practical time is influenced by data movement and locality as well as operation counts.
feedback.details: This does not prove sorting always wins; it disproves the universal hash claim.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.cache_changes_big_o: They affect constants and practical throughput, not the abstract comparison count.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_random: Many sorting and scan phases are locality-friendly.

items.prompt: Memory is limited, input mutation is legal, and only duplicate existence is needed. Which fact can make sorting preferable?

options.text: An in-place sort and scan can use much less auxiliary memory than an O(n)-entry hash set.
A set uses no auxiliary memory.
Sorting is free when mutation is legal.
Duplicates can only be detected through hashing.

feedback.reason: A slower asymptotic time may be the legal choice under a stricter space budget.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_no_time: It still costs O(n log n) under comparison sorting.

items.prompt: The output must list groups in ascending key order. Why can sorting be a better fit?

options.text: Sorting creates the exact order and adjacency consumed by the output.
A hash map automatically iterates in ascending key order.
O(n log n) is definitionally faster than O(n).
Output requirements make memory irrelevant.

feedback.reason: The sort is not overhead when its order is a required result structure.
feedback.details: A hash path may need to sort keys later, so the full cost can be similar or larger.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.nlogn_faster_definition: Asymptotic growth says the opposite for large n under aligned models.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.memory_irrelevant: Memory still belongs in the comparison.

items.prompt: Keys have a valid total order but deliberately no compatible hash function. What happens to the universal hash claim?

options.text: Hashing is not a legal candidate, while sorting remains available.
Any arbitrary object address can replace semantic hashing.
No strategy can process the keys.
Stringifying keys is always semantically safe.

feedback.reason: Correctness and legal key operations precede speed comparison.
feedback.details: A strategy that cannot implement the domain relation is not made valid by its expected complexity.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.invent_hash: It may violate equality compatibility and stability.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_strings: String encodings may collide or fail to match domain equality.

items.prompt: Input is already sorted and the task needs one grouping scan. Which conclusion rejects the heuristic?

options.text: The sorting-based representation is already available, so a linear run scan can beat building extra hash state.
Hashing must rebuild because expected O(n) always wins.
The array must be sorted again before every scan.
Equal-run grouping cannot use existing order.

feedback.reason: Existing preprocessing changes the starting point of the workload.
feedback.details: Ignoring supplied order creates a biased comparison.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_rebuild: Both paths are O(n), but the hash path allocates unused state.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_again: The precondition says it is already sorted.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.groups_need_hash: That is exactly what sorted adjacency enables.

items.prompt: For small n, why can a simple sort or scan outperform hash-table setup?

options.text: Allocation, hashing, bucket initialization, and indirection can dominate small workloads.
Big-O analysis is mathematically false.
Every hash operation is quadratic.
Sorting small arrays is always O(1) asymptotically.

feedback.reason: Asymptotic dominance does not specify every finite crossover point.
feedback.details: After correctness, implementation simplicity and constants can guide small-input choices.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.big_o_false: It remains valid for growth, not exact small-instance timing.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_quadratic: Expected operations can still be constant.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_constant: n remains a variable in the complexity model.

items.prompt: A service accepts attacker-controlled keys and requires predictable worst-case response. Which concern weakens a conventional hash-table choice?

options.text: Adversarial collisions can violate the expected constant-time assumption.
Sorting can never receive adversarial input.
Hash tables do not process keys.
Expected bounds are identical to worst-case bounds.

feedback.reason: The input model and guarantee level must match the analysis.
feedback.details: Protected hashing, balanced buckets, or deterministic sorting may be considered depending on the system contract.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_no_attack: It can, but deterministic comparison bounds remain analyzable.

items.prompt: Hashing a large structured key is expensive, while comparing keys often stops after a cheap prefix. What does this show?

options.text: The number of operations must be combined with the actual cost of hashing and comparison.
Expected O(1) means hash computation is free.
Early-ending comparisons make sorting incorrect.
All key operations have identical constants by definition.

feedback.reason: Expected linear versus n log n counts are meaningful only under a stated primitive-cost model.
feedback.details: Expensive hashing can move the practical crossover substantially.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_free: O(1) probe count does not erase key processing.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.keys_constant: The cost model may distinguish them.

items.prompt: Which final decision rule is invalid?

options.text: Choose hashing whenever its headline expected asymptotic term is smaller, without checking the result or assumptions.
Reject strategies that cannot preserve required order, indices, or legal key operations.
Include preprocessing, queries, updates, output, and memory.
Match expected or deterministic guarantees to the environment.

feedback.reason: A strategy label is not a proof.
feedback.details: The universal heuristic fails because it omits correctness and most of the cost model.

items.prompt: Which are valid counterexamples to "hash maps are always faster than sorting"? Select all that apply.

options.text: The input is already sorted and only a linear run scan is needed.
The result requires ascending groups, so hash keys would need later sorting.
Mutation is legal but O(n) auxiliary table memory is not.
Millions of arbitrary-order equality queries over static hashable keys.

feedback.reason: A universal claim is refuted by any legal workload where sorting better satisfies the contract or resource limits.
feedback.details: The equality-heavy workload illustrates that hashing can still be the right conditional choice.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.equality_many: This is a strong hash case, not a counterexample.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.tight_memory: In-place sorting may fit the constraint.

items.prompt: A design review is considering a hash table. Which facts are positive evidence for that choice? Select all that apply.

options.text: The workload is dominated by equality membership, counts, or key-to-value updates.
Keys have a compatible and adequately distributed cheap hash.
Ordered traversal is not required.
The workload requires deterministic range traversal by key.

feedback.reason: Rejecting the universal heuristic does not mean rejecting hashing.
feedback.details: It means stating the conditions under which hashing is justified.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.deterministic_range: A plain hash table does not provide it.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.equality_state: Hash state directly serves these operations.

items.prompt: A comparison-sort path is still legal. Which facts make its ordered representation worth paying for? Select all that apply.

options.text: The output or queries consume key order or adjacency.
Keys are comparable but not legally hashable.
Input mutation is legal and lower auxiliary memory is required.
The first repeated arrival must be reported before the stream ends.

feedback.reason: Sorting is justified by ordered semantics, legal key operations, or resource tradeoffs.
feedback.details: Online arrival-sensitive queries favor incremental lookup state.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.online_first_repeat: Offline sorting cannot preserve this online stopping point directly.

items.prompt: Which evidence is needed for a defensible strategy claim? Select all that apply.

options.text: The exact observable result and preservation requirements.
Preprocessing, query, update, output, memory, and key-operation costs.
Expected, average, amortized, or worst-case assumptions.
Only the presence of the word map or sorted.

feedback.reason: A strategy conclusion is conditional on semantics and assumptions.
feedback.details: Naming a familiar data structure is not a proof.

items.prompt: Input is already sorted. Compare grouping equal runs directly with rebuilding a hash frequency table for n values and k distinct keys under expected O(1) hashing.
constraints: The run scan stores only current key and count.
The hash table stores one entry per distinct key.

feedback.reason: Both paths are linear in time under the stated model, so the hash path is not asymptotically faster.
feedback.details: The existing sorted representation lets the run scan use less auxiliary state and emit ordered groups directly.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: Reject universal claims: choose from correctness, guarantees, memory, ordering, mutation, locality, key operations, and complete workload cost.
